question_structure.context: The working principle of the electromagnetic railgun is that the projectile to be launched can move freely between two parallel rails and maintain good contact with the rails. Current flows in from one rail, passes through the conductive projectile, and then flows back through the other rail. The rail current generates a magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the rails at the projectile (which can be considered as a uniform magnetic field). The electrified projectile is then propelled at high speed along the rails due to the Lorentz force acting on it. Xiao Ming purchased a railgun model online, with a rail length of \( L = 0.5 \, \mathrm{m} \), a parallel rail spacing of \( d = 0.02 \, \mathrm{m} \), a projectile mass of \( m = 0.02 \, \mathrm{kg} \), a rail current of \( I = 10 \, \mathrm{A} \), and a magnetic field strength of \( B = 1 \, \mathrm{T} \). All resistances are neglected.
question_structure.sub_question_1: What is the magnitude of the acceleration of the projectile moving along the trajectory?
question_structure.sub_question_2: What is the magnitude of the projectile's exit velocity?
question_structure.sub_question_3: What is the power of the electromagnetic railgun when the projectile is launched?

sub_question_1.step_1: From Newton's second law, we have $BId = ma$, solving for $a$ gives $a = 10\mathrm{m/s^2}$.

sub_question_2.step_2: From the work-energy theorem, we have $BIgL = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - 0$, solving for $v$ gives $v = \sqrt{10}\mathrm{m/s}$.

sub_question_3.step_3: The power of the electromagnetic railgun when the projectile is launched is $P = BIgv = \frac{\sqrt{10}}{5}\mathrm{W}$.

image_captions: The image shows two parallel horizontal black lines representing a track. To the left, there is an arrow pointing left on the top line and another arrow pointing left on the bottom line. In the center, a grey rectangular object is placed vertically, with an upward pointing arrow on it. To the right of the grey object, there is a rightward pointing arrow above the lower track line. Beyond this, dashed light blue lines extend outward to form a triangular shape, pointing toward the text "parallel tracks", which translates to "parallel track".
